```md
## FEW-SHOT STYLE PACK (ORIGINAL TEXT)

### A) Jhace — Wet Trace at Blackgate (≈130 words)
Rain pinholed the chalk the first time, lying like it always did. Jhace knelt, wiped the board on his thigh, and traced again with the stick held sideways so the line fattened instead of skipping. The deck’s grain took the mark; two Veins crossed where they had no business crossing. He lifted a hand. Brighlee caught it, swung the blue flag across the lane. Stall-keepers groaned like he’d stolen a bell from them. He didn’t argue. He set the feeler iron against the bracket and waited for the note under the rain. There—an off-ring, thin as a cut. “Press two spans back,” he said. Two short bell taps answered. The puddle under the eaves rippled to the rhythm, and the deck remembered how to hold.

### B) Tiffani — Wedge Before Veil (≈140 words)
The corridor complained in hairlines, a spider’s whisper along the pane when night air knifed through. Tiffani pressed the wedge with the heel of her hand until the draught narrowed to a thread. Then she Veiled the glass: damp cloth laid from center to edge, breath on a count of three, a pause, and another pass. The gloss dulled at the margins where it liked to crack. A floor hand hovered with a blanket and good intentions. “No,” she said, not unkind. “Blankets smother. This listens.” She tapped the edge with a knuckle; the sound came back honest. On the ledger she wrote it plain—**wedge before veil**—and drew the little wedge glyph so a sleepy apprentice couldn’t miss it. Outside, bells talked to each other. Inside, the pane kept quiet.

### C) Micro-intimacy — After Posting (≈90 words)
They met in the lee of the posting board while the ink decided to dry. Jhace’s hands were still hot from the pin; Tiffani’s fingers smelled like damp cloth and salt. “Close?” he asked. “Stay,” she said, already leaning. The kiss wasn’t long. It didn’t have to be. One bell called a brace somewhere, and the board hummed a little under their shoulders, iron singing to wood. They broke clean. “Later,” he said. “When the city lets us,” she said, and tucked a loose hair back under her scarf like that was a promise.

---

## QUALITY CHECK (RUN AFTER EACH CHAPTER)
- Did I show the **operation** in tactile detail?  
- Did I show a **cost**?  
- Did I create or update a **public artifact**?  
- Did I avoid all **red-lines**?  
- Does the **closer image** land without purple prose?  
- Did I keep romance adult, consent-clear, and subordinate to work?

---

## BACK-BLURB TEMPLATE (FILL AFTER CH15)
*On a wounded world where bridges hold cities and glass keeps bodies whole, two workers learn to move the lines beneath everything: he in iron and span, she in heat and breath. When rain lies and glass sings, their hands make new rules the city can follow. “Lines That Hold” is a story of craft, love, and the kind of paperwork that keeps people alive.*

---

## TITLE PAGE
**Lines That Hold**  
A Te’Oga Novella  
Words: ~15k–22k (15 chapters)

---

## FINAL REMINDERS
- Don’t get clever. Earn every small win with process, cost, and a posted rule.  
- Use the **icon card** as a character: when it changes, the world changes.  
- The last page is quiet on purpose. Let competence be the climax.
```

[USER]
This was the first response

Lines That Hold

Te’Oga — Novella 1

Author: —
Places: Grindheim (Underface, span/forge); Strathmoor (Outer Crown, kiln/clinic)
Institutions: Veinwrights’ Conclave; Surveyors’ Chain; Mercers’ Table; Couriers’ Bell; Healers of the Line
Day Measure: 8 bells
Forge Heat: Bells 1–9
Glass Shade: Steps 0–12

Chapter 1: Wet Trace at Blackgate

Jhace took the ladder rungs three at a time and stepped into the Underface air that tasted like coins. Bell 2 chimed through the ribs of Grindheim, a short steel note that vibrated the teeth. Blackgate waited ahead, a square mouth in the wall where the span narrowed to a choke. A queue of carts pressed to the right, teamsters standing on brake boards. To the left, the Posting board wore its Tempermark pin and the usual icons: droplet, wedge, hand, chain. The droplet told the crowd what they didn’t want to hear: wet work at the gate, keep to the outer rail, mind your boots, no sparks.

Tiffani stood already at the cleave, veil case open on her knee. She’d marked a half-circle with chalk, neat as a draftsman, so the crowd pressure wouldn’t chew into their backs. Her hair was wrapped tight under a weld-cloth, and her hands moved like she was measuring without tools, thumb to knuckle to nail. Veld hands counted without thinking. Jhace liked that. He liked that her kit lay in straight lines, that she color-coded her threads and taped shade-step cards to the inside lid of her case. Workers who made straight lines kept people alive.

“Home line there,” she said, nodding at the tie-off ring bolted waist-high to the gate’s post. “One hand on it.”

“One hand on home,” he said, because saying the clause out loud made the body obey when the veer came.

He wore fray bands on both wrists and across the meat of his thumb. Fresh cloth. He checked them again. He checked the Press sledge by habit, then shoved it aside because today was Trace, not Press. The Surveyors’ Chain had sent the fault note hours ago: Blackgate’s Vein had gone light and quick, Threft slipping along the wall seam in a thin veil, trying to cut under the roadway. If it cut deep and found a hollow, the Harrow would run the whole span and dump carts and bodies into the Underface drop. Wet Trace along the gate plate would carry heat into that skittering line, slow it to the burrow path, persuade it back under the strap bars. Persuasion first. Force only if persuasion failed. The Conclave taught that order hard, because Overbind made a clean disaster.

Brighlee, Conclave clerk with Quill-stamp authority, arrived with her blue ledger, boots chewing grit. She didn’t look up from the form. “Work order nine-three-two, Wet Trace, Blackgate. Setter: Jhace brast. Glass: Tiffani veld. No HALT. Witness me.”

“Witnessed,” both of them said. She Tempermarked the card, droplet icon, Bell 2, and clipped it to the board. Paper first, then metal. Paper was how the city remembered what had been done.

Tiffani slid her Lume rod along the seam and switched Shade to two, then three. The thin light pierced the metal’s skin and came back with a blur. She listened with her mouth slightly open, as if Threft had a tone she could catch with her teeth. Maybe veld ears did. She tapped twice, brace taps at Bell 2. The sound climbed and feathered. “It’s quick,” she said. “Not mean, just quick. Wants the short route.”

“Short route is under their wheels.” Jhace spat to clear the metal taste. “We give it the long.”

He laid his kit out: trace stick with copper nib, water bladder with a threaded cap, two wedges cut at seven degrees, not eight. Wedge-before-veil. The rule saved wrists. He ran a breath count, ten in and ten out, to settle the little tremor in his pinky. Old fray shimmered in nerves for bells after work, and Blackgate had knocked him hard last season. He could feel the way his hands remembered the jolt.

Crowd noise pushed and thinned. Someone called for hurry in the cart line. The Posting icons did their work; the brake-men shifted their weight so the load sat quiet. Jhace nodded once, to them, to the board, to the wall, and then took the home line in his left hand. He pressed one wedge into the seam two spans north of the gate’s mouth, just deep enough to feel resistance. He didn’t want to split; he wanted to suggest. He snugged the wedge with a thumb heel. The seam opened a hair. Air came cool against his knuckles.

Tiffani watched the Lume’s feedback. “You’ve got it looking,” she said, small smile. “Another wedge at the shoulder.”

He set the second wedge at the shoulder, where the seam turned and tried to climb under the bridge stone. “Half-beat Slake,” she said.

“Half-beat Slake,” he answered, and thumbed the bladder’s cap. Water at Bell 5 felt warm in the palm, because the Underface never forgot to heat the fingers that gripped it. He wet the copper nib, flicked once to clear bubbles, and set it to the face.

Wet Trace moved slow. The nib had to drink in a constant feed. Dry nib scored and invited fracture. He laid the line with his shoulder, not his wrist, elbow low, keeping the pressure a steady two fingers’ worth. The Vein under the metal sensed heat through the water, rolled against it like a lazy fish. The Trace line would give it a good bank to push against. He could feel, in the bones, how the Vein’s quickness steadied when the metal drank.

“Shade three to four,” Tiffani said. Her Lume showed a thicker pulse, and she shifted the rod angle by a finger, then another. “Veil after the turn. No white. We keep it honest.”

“No white,” he muttered. Whitequench made glass lie. Velds didn’t break that rule, not if they wanted to keep standing with the Healers when a fray went bad. He laid the Trace through the turn, the water turning to steam under his glove. He rolled his shoulder through the ache. Cart axles chirped behind. The Blackgate mouth breathed. Threft carried the city, and the city swayed on that breath.

The line took, and he felt the nib draw as if the wall itself had leaned into his hand. Good. He kept to the count, slow and repeatable. Half-beat Slake. Wet and set, wet and set. The nib squeaked when the copper hit a grit island and jumped. He froze the wrist, let the shoulder hold hard, then breathed it through and recovered. Error scrapes could cut a place for trouble to settle. He smoothed it with a second pass and felt Tiffani’s knuckles touch his elbow, firm.

“Close?” she asked.

“Stay,” he said, and her hand warmed through his sleeve. Not for the crowd. Not for show. For work. Consent in the lane so the body could stop bracing. The tremor eased.

At the gate mouth, the Threft made a petty run for the wheel cut, the shallow groove where iron met stone. The Vein liked edges. Everything in the Underface liked an edge. Jhace pinched the nib flat, spread the wet along a wider arc, and then lifted for a breath to let the water stand as a beaded bow. He brought the nib back to the center and pushed.

Tiffani’s veil kit clicked. Shade six sat ready in its case, a skin as thin as onion and tough like a hymn you didn’t admit you knew by heart. Not yet. Wet first, wedge first, then small veil. Big veils waited for bigger sins.

“Brighlee,” Tiffani said without looking. “Log the wedge set.” Brighlee scratched it into the ledger with a shorthand that sounded like a wire brush. Paper was policy. If they ate it wrong later, someone could look and see where the eating started.

Bell 3 chimed, longer. Sweat ran under Jhace’s fray bands and burned, just a bite. He ran the trace for a second length, adding a shallow counterline along the upper lip to stop any climb. Counterline wasn’t Countermark; that would be a joint with glass later and he didn’t want to steal work from future bells. He just wanted the Vein to forget the stairs and remember the hall.

The smell shifted. Not rust. Not steam. The small sharp of Hollow nibbling itself somewhere under the plates. Jhace tasted it at the back of his tongue. Threft had moods. He didn’t call them that where apprentices could hear, but brasts who lied about the way Veins pulled and sagged died early. He changed hand position, lowered his stance, and ran out the last of the arc in a steady curve.

“Lume says you’ve got it,” Tiffani murmured. “It’s feeding back into the strap bars. Good. Give me the lip there.”

He filled the lower lip with a heavier wet and flattened the nib. The water took a turn, left to right, like it hit a bend in a river. The gate seemed to exhale. The cart line took a step forward, then stopped at the droplet icon without protest. Paper had done its job, again.

Tiffani pulled a small veil, just palm-width, and set Shade to one. She breathed on it to warm, pivoted the Lume so the reflection tracked his line, and laid the veil over the beaded bow he had left. Her fingers pressed in measured taps along the edge, seven taps, then another seven. “Annealshift?” she asked, automatic.

“Zero. Let the wall do it.” Wet against Veil; the two would slake together. No forced shifts. No White.

She nodded. “Veil in. Trace held.” Her gaze kept chasing the returning light like a cat tracking string. “Stay with your home.”

He squeezed the line ring. “With it.”

Brighlee passed him the stamp. He could have waited, finished the last short run first, but Blackgate had a memory for intent. Put the mark into it early and the wall cooperated. He pressed the setter’s stamp into the small plate to the right of the seam, the plate already crowded with old solves, old names, old dates. The stamp bit deep. The face showed a simple cleave with a single beaded line under it. Wet Trace icon. His initials sat small in the corner. Not vanity. Audit.

Tiffani flicked a finger against her Lume. “Hear that?” she asked.

“What.”

“It’s not singing at you anymore. It’s humming. That’s the sound they put babies to sleep with up in Strathmoor. Field calm.”

He didn’t argue. He didn’t look up into her eyes because he’d keep looking. He watched the line. He finished the last span, sealed it with a wet kiss of the nib to the wedge heads, then twisted each wedge a quarter-turn to let the wood find its own new squeeze.

The Conclave taught the wet-trace rule in the first week and kept teaching it until apprentices stopped trying to go fast and dry. Dry scratched clean lines. Dry pleased the eye. Dry got workers killed, because Threft hated dry, slid off it, tore around the edges and came back mean. Wet lines didn’t look like much. They darkened and went dull and then, hours later, they were the only lines left.

“Cost?” Tiffani asked, when the last drip fell clean.

He held up his hands. Fray bands had gone gray at the edge where steam had chewed the dye. Ear ring sang a small circle. He wiggled fingers. “Tremor minimal. Thirst.”

She opened her water bottle, pressed it to his palm. “Clinic bell if the ring keeps past 4.”

“Policy,” he said, and drank. It tasted like it had hit glass. Veld water always did.

Brighlee tore a copy of the work order and slid it into the metal mouth of the Posting board, under the droplet icon. She stamped the corner with Tempermark, the little raised ridge that told fingers what paper said when eyes were busy elsewhere. “Posted,” she said. She lifted the blue ledger so the crowd could see the droplet icon, the Bell number, the names, the mark. The teamsters shifted weight off brake boards. Paper turned bodies. Money would move later at the Mercers’ Table, coin transferred by weight, not hope.

Jhace rechecked the gate face. The wet line sat like a seam of darker metal, almost nothing. The wedges held. The Veil lay without bubbles. Tiffani eased the Lume up to Shade two once more and smiled without showing teeth. “Blackgate doesn’t want to bite today,” she said.

“It’ll remember we made it look good,” Jhace said. He wanted to reach for her wrist and feel the pulse there. He didn’t. Work took the time it took. Bells later were for the other thing.

They unhooked from the home line. Tiffani peeled her veil case closed, slid the Lume in, and tucked a thread of hair back under the weld cloth. Brighlee moved away without ceremony, already writing the next form as if the world could be fixed by ink if you just wrote fast enough. Maybe sometimes it could.

The crowd flowed. The first cart creaked through the gate, iron rim a hand-width from Jhace’s trace. The rim hit the slight bow of water he’d left and didn’t squeal. The teamster looked down at the line and then up at Jhace with the nod that meant you did the thing and I saw you.

Jhace picked up his kit. The ear ring eased from a circle to a dot. He could breathe without counting. He looked one last time at the trace.

Public change: the droplet icon on the Posting board, their names, the stamp pressed into the plate, the wedges like small teeth set into the seam. Concrete image: the wet line along Blackgate’s face, a single continuous bead shining under the Underface light, thin steam lifting, the droplet icon above it catching sweat from a worker’s glove and turning darker by a shade.

He wiped his copper nib clean, threaded the cap on the bladder until it bit, and felt the gate breathe with the city. Wet line held. The gate kept its place. They moved on.

Continuity Ledger — after Chapter 1
	•	Posted clauses (current):
wet-trace rule; wedge-before-veil; half-beat Slake; brace taps (Bells 2–3); one hand on home line; setter must witness clear.
	•	Artifacts:
Tempermarked droplet icon card posted at Blackgate (Bell 2); setter’s stamp on Blackgate plate (Wet Trace icon + initials); Conclave work order 932 (copy in Posting board); wedges set at seam shoulder and north span; Tiffani’s palm-width Shade 1 veil laid over beaded bow.
	•	Costs:
Jhace: fray band wear; mild ear ring; thirst. Tiffani: dry mouth; minor finger heat. Time: Bell 2 to early Bell 3. Coin: ledger to clear later at Mercers’ Table. Clinic: none yet; observe ring into Bell 4 per policy.

[ASSISTANT]
Alright, here’s the verdict on your Ch.1 draft: it’s 90% on-model and already wears the right boots. But you tripped a couple wires I told you to avoid, and you’re sprinkling meta after the scene like parsley. I’m going to be blunt so the next passes actually move the needle.

# What’s working
- **Procedural spine is solid.** Wet Trace → wedge-before-veil → half-beat Slake → audit stamp → public posting. That’s the series DNA.
- **POV discipline holds.** Tight third on Jhace with Tiffani as competent counterpoint. No head-hop whiplash.
- **Paperwork matters.** Work order number, Tempermarked card, setter’s plate. That’s exactly how the city “remembers.”
- **Consent micro-beat:** “Close?” “Stay.” In-lane, work-anchored, not horny. Perfect.

# Problems to fix (non-negotiable)
1. **Ban the “singing/humming” business.** You promised no singing/harmonics vocabulary for magic cues. Lines to fix:
   - “It’s not **singing** at you anymore. It’s **humming**.”  
     Replace with: “It’s **steady now**. The line’s **quiet**.”
   - “iron **singing** to wood.”  
     Replace with: “iron **settling** to wood” or “iron **answering** wood.”
   - “tough like a **hymn** you didn’t admit you knew by heart.”  
     Replace with: “tough like cured skin” or “thin as onion, stubborn as hide.”

2. **Kill the in-text meta.**  
   The “Public change:” and “Continuity Ledger — after Chapter 1” sections cannot live inside the published prose. Keep them in your behind-the-scenes ledger or as end-notes to yourself. Readers shouldn’t see your checklist.

3. **Icon canon drift.**  
   You used a **hand** icon on the posting board. Canonical icon set for N1 didn’t include a hand. Use the **home-line ring** glyph (a small ring emblem) or stick to wedge/arrow/droplet/bell + Conclave quill block. Fix the line: “the usual icons: droplet, wedge, **ring**, chain.”

4. **Echoes and filler.**  
   You repeat “He didn’t argue” and some double-tells (“Paper first, then metal. Paper was how the city remembered…”). Trim once; the second sentence is dead weight.

5. **One metaphor too many.**  
   “Underface air that tasted like coins” is great. Keep it. Ditch the “hymn,” as above. Stay in metal/glass/water/heat.

6. **Minor lore hygiene.**  
   “Counterline wasn’t Countermark” is fine, but make sure it’s clear it’s **still** within legal practice. A tiny clause like “legal counterline” prevents a later reader from thinking he freelanced a taboo.

# Surgical line edits (drop-in replacements)

- **Singing/hymn fixes**
  - “It’s not singing at you anymore. It’s humming.”  
    → “It’s steady now,” she said. “The line’s quiet.”
  - “iron singing to wood.”  
    → “iron settling into wood.”
  - “tough like a hymn you didn’t admit you knew by heart.”  
    → “thin as onion skin, stubborn as hide.”

- **Icon set**
  - “droplet, wedge, hand, chain.”  
    → “droplet, wedge, ring, chain.”

- **Echo trim**
  - Keep: “He didn’t argue.”  
    Cut the second “He didn’t argue.” later in the page.

- **Meta removal**
  - Delete from “Public change: …” down through the “Continuity Ledger…” block in the *reader* version. Keep that content in your private ledger file.

- **Legal clarity**
  - “He filled the lower lip with a heavier wet and flattened the nib… Counterline wasn’t Countermark…”  
    → Add “legal” once: “a **legal counterline** wasn’t Countermark…”

# Micro craft notes (tighten without neutering)
- “He ran a breath count, ten in and ten out” → consider “He counted to ten on each breath.” Shorter, same beat.
- “The Conclave taught that order hard” → “The Conclave beat that order into apprentices.” Harsher, in-world.
- “Tiffani’s veil kit clicked” → great, keep; it’s tactile. Maybe lose “like a hymn” right after.

# Chapter-end rule check

- **Concrete image:** “a single continuous bead shining under Underface light” with a glove-sweat darkening the icon. ✔  
- **No HALT used:** good. Keep your one HALT for a later chapter in N1.

- **Cost shown:** fray band wear, ear ring, thirst. ✔  
- **Public artifact changed:** posted droplet card, plate stamp. ✔  
- **Tiny lawful beauty:** puddle rings to bell taps. You used this once. Don’t overuse it later. ✔

# Continuity/grit nits
- “Press sledge” is fine as kit, even if not used this operation. You correctly shoved it aside so the scene doesn’t confuse the Pressline with Wet Trace.
- “Whitequench made glass lie.” Good reminder. Keep it once; don’t chant it chapterly.
- “Home line there… one hand on it.” Perfect. Keep repeating the clause across the book; make it culture.

**Replace with:**

Pick one of these cold opens and go:

- Nuke the meta blocks from the reader version.

You clean those, Chapter 1 is print-ready for what this series promises: competence, cost, and a city that behaves because someone wrote it down.

## 2) Chapter 1 — Wet Trace at Blackgate (tight cut, reader version)

Jhace climbed into Underface air that tasted like coins and crossed to Blackgate, a square mouth where the span pinched. Bell Two shook the ribs of Grindheim. The posting board wore its Tempermark pin and the usual icons: droplet, wedge, ring, chain. The droplet meant wet work, slow feet, no sparks.

Tiffani already had a chalk half-circle to keep the press off their backs. Veil case open on her knee. Hair wrapped. Hands moving like she measured without tools. Workers who laid straight lines kept people alive.

“Home line there,” she said, nodding to the ring bolted waist-high on the post. “One hand on it.”

“One hand on home,” he said, because saying it makes the body obey when the veer comes.

Brighlee arrived boots-first, ledger under her arm. “Work order nine-three-two. Wet Trace. Blackgate. Setter: Jhace brast. Glass: Tiffani veld. No HALT. Witness me.”

“Witnessed,” they said together. She Tempermarked the card, droplet icon, Bell Two, clipped it to the board. Paper first. Paper is how the city remembers.

The Surveyors’ note had said Blackgate’s Vein had gone quick along the wall seam, trying to cut under the roadway. If it found a hollow, Harrow would run the span and pitch carts into the drop. Wet Trace would carry heat by water into that skittering line and bank it back under the strap bars. Persuade first. Force only if persuasion failed. Overbind makes a clean disaster.

He checked his fray bands and laid out the kit: trace stick with copper nib, water bladder, two wedges cut at seven degrees. Wedge-before-veil. The rule saves wrists.

Tiffani slid her Lume along the seam and stepped Shade from two to three. Light came back blurred. She listened with her mouth slightly open, catching more than sight. “It’s quick,” she said. “Not mean. Wants the short route.”

“Short route is under their wheels.” Jhace spat metal taste. “We give it the long.”

He took the home ring in his left hand and pressed a wedge into the seam two spans north of the gate, just deep enough for a grip. He didn’t want to split. He wanted to suggest. A second wedge at the shoulder where the seam tried to climb.

“Half-beat Slake,” she said.

“Half-beat Slake,” he answered, wetting the copper nib and setting it to the face.

Wet Trace moves slow. The nib drinks constant and heat rides the water. Dry nib scores and tempts fracture. He laid the line with his shoulder, not his wrist, elbow low, pressure steady—about two fingers’ worth. The Vein under the plate pressed back like a lazy fish turning toward a current. He felt it steady.

“Shade three to four,” Tiffani said. Her Lume showed thicker pulse. She shifted rod angle a finger at a time. “Veil after the turn. No white.”

“No white,” he said. Whitequench makes glass lie. He ran the Trace through the turn. Steam whispered under his glove. Cart axles chirped behind. The Blackgate mouth breathed with the city.

The nib squeaked over a grit island and jumped. He froze the wrist, let the shoulder carry, breathed through, passed again. Error scrapes attract trouble. Tiffani’s knuckles touched his elbow—firm, not fussing.

“Close?” she asked.

“Stay,” he said. Her hand warmed through cloth. Consent in the lane makes the body stop bracing. The tremor eased.

At the mouth the Vein made a petty run for the wheel cut, as Veins will at edges. He pinched the nib flat, spread a wider wet arc, lifted for a breath to leave a beaded bow, then pushed the center again.

Tiffani set a palm-width veil to Shade One and breathed on it to warm. She pivoted the Lume until reflection tracked his line and laid the veil over that beaded bow. Seven measured taps along the edge, then seven more. “Annealshift?”

“Zero. Let the wall do it.” Wet against veil would slake together. No forced shifts.

“Good,” she murmured. “Give me the lip.”

He filled the lower lip with heavier wet, flattened the nib. The water took a turn left to right, as if the plate had a bend. The gate exhaled. The cart line answered the droplet icon without theatrics. Paper had done its part.

Bell Three chimed, longer. Sweat burned a little under the bands. He ran the Trace a second length, added a **legal** counterline along the upper lip to keep the climb honest. Not Countermark. Not a joint with glass. Just a reminder to stay on the hall and forget the stairs.

Smell changed—metal warm, steam clean, and a faint thinness that meant Hollow somewhere under plates. He tasted it at the back of his tongue, lowered his stance, finished the arc steady. Tiffani tracked the returning light, head cocked. “It’s steady now,” she said. “The line’s quiet.”

He didn’t look away from the seam. He finished the last span, kissed the wedge heads with a wet touch, and turned each wedge a quarter to let the wood find new grip.

Brighlee lifted the ledger so the crowd could see droplet icon, bell, names. The teamsters shifted off their brake boards like someone had unhanded their ribs.

“Mark,” she said.

He pressed his setter stamp into the small plate to the right of the seam. The plate’s face was a crowd of old solves and old names. His mark showed a simple cleave with a beaded line. Audit, not vanity.

Tiffani kept her Lume at Shade Two for a breath more. “Hear that?” she asked, meaning the absence. “The chatter’s gone. It’s behaving.”

He finished the short run to the turn and let the wet line darken. Steam lifted in a straight curl and then nothing. He thumbed the cap onto the bladder until it bit.

“Cost?” she asked.

He flexed his hands. Bands gone gray at the edges where steam chewed dye. Ear ring a small dot. “Tremor minimal. Thirst.”

She pressed her water into his palm. “Clinic bell if the ring keeps past Four.”

“Policy,” he said, drinking. Veld water always tasted faintly of glass.

Brighlee slid a copy of the work order into the mouth of the posting board under the droplet icon and Tempermarked the corner, the small ridge that tells fingers what paper says when eyes are busy. “Posted,” she said.

“Span’s yours,” a teamster muttered, not unkind. The front wheel eased over the traced arc. It hit the little bow of water and didn’t squeal. He looked down, then up, and gave them the old nod: you did the thing and I saw you.

Tiffani peeled the veil and coiled it in the case, wiped a thread of hair back into its wrap. Jhace set the copper nib to the cloth and ran it clean. He wanted the feel of her pulse under his thumb. He wanted to keep his hands still. Work takes the time it takes.

They unhooked from the home ring together. The board stood with its new droplet posted, names in ink, the plate stamped, wedges seated as small teeth in the seam. Air came up from the Underface warm against his wrist. He checked the line one last time.

The wet seam lay like a seam of darker metal, almost nothing. A continuous bead under the sour light. Thin steam lifted, then ended. Someone’s glove brushed the droplet icon; sweat darkened the Tempermark by a shade. That was the whole point: public change you could touch. The gate breathed. The line held. They moved on.

---

## 3) Private continuity notes (do not print in the prose)
- Posted clauses (current): wet-trace rule; wedge-before-veil; half-beat Slake; brace taps (Bells 2–3); one hand on home line; setter must witness clear.

You want a scalpel? Fine. I cut it lean, kept the muscle, tossed the fluff. Opening compressed, middle tightened, wrap trimmed. Ledger moved out of the prose where it belongs.

⸻

Chapter 1: Wet Trace at Blackgate (compressed)

Bell 2 rang short across the Underface. Jhace came up the ladder and into Blackgate’s choke where carts queued tight to the right and the Posting board sat left with its Tempermark pin and droplet icon. Wet work. Keep to the outer rail. No sparks.

Tiffani already had a chalk half-circle behind them so the crowd wouldn’t eat their backs. Veil case open on her knee. Lume rod in her fist. Weld cloth over her hair. She pointed to the tie-off ring bolted waist-high to the post. “One hand on home.”

“One hand on home,” he echoed, because the body obeyed what the mouth said.

Brighlee from the Conclave arrived breathing paper. “Work order nine-three-two. Wet Trace, Blackgate. Setter: Jhace brast. Glass: Tiffani veld. No HALT.” She Tempermarked the droplet card and clipped it to the board. Icon, Bell number, names. Witnessed. The carts settled an inch. Paper moves bodies.

The Chain’s fault note had said quick Vein under the gate plate, running light along a seam and sniffing for a hollow. If it found depth, the Harrow would undercut the roadway and send carts into the Underface. Persuasion first. Force later if you liked funerals.

Jhace staged only what he needed: two wedges cut at seven degrees, trace stick with copper nib, bladder threaded tight. Wedge-before-veil. Half-beat Slake. Brace taps only at Bells 2–3. He checked his fray bands and set his left hand to the home ring.

Tiffani laid Lume light along the seam and edged the Shade from two to three. Thin glare came back with a blur and a fast pulse. She listened with her mouth a little open. “Quick, not mean. Wants the short route.”

“Short route is under their wheels,” Jhace said. “We give it the long.”

He pressed the first wedge two spans north of the gate’s mouth until the wood met resistance, snugged it with his thumb heel, then set the second at the shoulder where the seam bent under the bridge stone. The seam opened a hair; cool air touched knuckles.

“Half-beat Slake,” she said.

He thumbed the bladder cap, wet the copper, flicked clear, and set the nib to the face. Wet Trace moved slow. Pressure from the shoulder, not the wrist. Two fingers’ worth, steady. Water turned to steam under his glove and carried heat to the Vein. The quickness steadied. The line took.

“Shade three to four,” Tiffani said, rolling the rod a finger or two to keep the reflection honest. “Veil after the turn. No white.”

“No white,” he said. Whitequench lied and got people hurt. He ran the line through the turn, keeping the wet beaded, not starved. A cart axle chirped behind them and then quieted when the teamster saw the icon and leaned on his brake board.

The copper squeaked across a grit island. Jhace froze his wrist, let the shoulder hold, breathed once, and smoothed the error on a second pass. Tiffani’s knuckles tapped his elbow, firm.

“Close?” she asked.

“Stay,” he said. Warmth through his sleeve. Consent in the lane so the body stopped bracing. His pinky tremor quit arguing.

At the gate mouth the Vein made a petty cut for the wheel groove. Veins loved edges. Jhace pinched the nib flat and spread the wet into a bow across the lip, then returned to the center and pushed it along. Tiffani watched the Lume feedback and nodded without wasting air.

“Log the wedge set,” she told Brighlee.

Scratch, scratch. Ledger caught it.

Bell 3 rang longer. Jhace added a shallow counterline along the upper lip to kill any climb. Not a joint, not a Countermark. Just a reminder: hallway, not stairs. The smell shifted to steam and light metal, no rust bite yet, no Hollow chew. Good.

“Give me the lower lip,” Tiffani said.

He pressed a heavier wet, and the water veered left to right like it had found a bend. The gate exhaled. Behind them, brake boards creaked but held. The Posting board’s droplet icon did the quiet work of law.

“Veil small,” she said, already warming a palm-width Shade 1 skin with her breath. She set it over the bow he’d left, taps in measured runs of seven along the edge. “Annealshift?”

“Zero,” he said. “Let the wall do it.”

“Veil in. Trace held.” She kept the Lume tracking the line.

Brighlee passed him the stamp. He didn’t wait. Blackgate liked a promise early. He pressed the setter’s mark into the crowded plate, Wet Trace icon with his initials small in the corner. Audit, not vanity.

“Listen,” Tiffani said.

“What.”

“It’s humming, not singing.” She kept the Lume steady. “Field calm. Strathmoor puts babies down with that.”

He finished the run, sealed the wedge heads with a wet kiss, and quarter-turned each wedge so the wood found its own squeeze. The Vein’s pulse under the plate slowed to a workday beat. Wet against Veil slaked as one.

“Cost?” she asked.

“Fray bands chewed at the edge. Ear ring a little circle. Thirst,” he said.

She handed her water bottle across. “Clinic if the ring holds past 4.”

“Policy.” He drank. Veld water always tasted faintly of glass.

Brighlee slid the stamped copy of the work order into the Posting board mouth beneath the droplet icon and lifted her blue ledger so the crowd could see the marks. Names, Bell, icon. The teamsters eased weight off their brakes and waited for her nod. Paper turned to motion.

Jhace checked the face. Wet line sat dull and even. Wedges held. No bubbles under the veil. Tiffani rolled Shade to two and smiled without teeth. “Blackgate’s not in a biting mood.”

“It remembers when we make it look good,” he said.

They unhooked from the home ring. Tiffani closed her case. Brighlee walked off already writing the next form, because ink thought it could outrun failure. Sometimes it did.

The first cart came through, iron rim a hand-width from the line. The rim rode the shallow bow and didn’t squeal. The teamster glanced down, then up, and gave Jhace the nod that meant: you did the thing, I saw you. The Underface breathed slow under the stone.

He wiped the copper clean, threaded the bladder cap until it bit, and stood with it all for one more count. The wet seam shone a single shade darker than the plate, steam lifting hair-thin. The droplet icon above it had picked up sweat from a worker’s glove and gone one shade darker itself.

Public change posted: droplet card Tempermarked at Blackgate with their names and Bell; setter’s stamp sunk into the plate. Concrete image: one continuous bead along the gate face, thin steam rising while the first cart crosses quiet.

⸻

What I cut or merged
•	Opening trimmed: fewer crowd textures, zero digressions on Tiffani’s color-coding, just enough kit to ground the craft.
•	Middle tightened: one clear statement of wet vs dry; fewer “he felt” redundancies; cut generalities about Conclave pedagogy.
•	Closing pruned: stamp, posting, cart confirmation; the rest moved to the ledger.

⸻

Continuity Ledger — separate
•	Posted clauses (current): wet-trace rule; wedge-before-veil; half-beat Slake; brace taps (Bells 2–3); one hand on home line; setter must witness clear.
•	Artifacts: Tempermarked droplet card at Blackgate (Bell 2) with names; setter’s stamp on Blackgate plate (Wet Trace + initials); work order 932 (copy in board); wedges at seam shoulder and north span; palm-width Shade 1 veil laid over beaded bow.
•	Costs: Jhace: fray band wear; mild ear ring; thirst. Tiffani: dry mouth; finger heat. Time: Bell 2 to early Bell 3. Coin: ledger to clear later at Mercers’ Table. Clinic: observe ring into Bell 4 per policy.

There. It lives at roughly your 1,400 target without sanding off the craft. Now we can move to Chapter 2 without carrying extra weight like a cart with a bad brake.

## Quick triage
- **Banned vocab violation:** you kept “**humming, not singing**.” That entire semantic field is off-limits. Use **steady/quiet/settled** instead.
- **Meta residue:** you still left a line starting with **“Public change posted: … Concrete image: …”** in the prose. That belongs in your private ledger, not the chapter.
- **Canon polish:** everything else stays in-bounds. Icon callouts look fine; tie-off **home ring** usage is good; “Whitequench lied” shows the taboo cleanly once and shuts up about it. Perfect.

- “**It’s humming, not singing.**” → “**It’s steady now. The line’s quiet.**”
- Delete the **“Public change posted: … Concrete image: …”** sentence pair from the narrative.

[USER]
This is what I think about the attempt at revision don’t fix it or anything I’m running this all back to you so we can adjust the custom got instructions to have these issue already addressed from the get go. So I can start from scratch 

**Alright. Scalpel time.**

## What’s weak:

**1. The Tiffani introduction is still doing too much work**

> “Veil case open on her knee. Lume rod in her fist. Weld cloth over her hair.”

This is a **list disguised as observation**. It reads like you’re checking boxes (introduce her tools, establish her competence) rather than letting her *do something* that shows both. You could collapse this into action:

> “Tiffani had the chalk circle down and was already reading the seam with her Lume when Jhace cleared the ladder.”

Same information, half the words, **active** instead of static.

**2. The “body obeyed what the mouth said” construction is clunky**

> “…because the body obeyed what the mouth said.”

This is **telling** us about a ritual when you should **show** the ritual working. Either:

- Cut the explanation entirely (just have him repeat it)
- Or make it **physical**: “His left hand moved to the ring before his brain caught up.”

The current version is exposition wearing a clever phrase.

**3. “Paper moves bodies” is trying too hard**

> “Paper moves bodies.”

This wants to be a killer line. It’s not. It’s **thesis statement**, not prose. You’re *telling* us the thematic point instead of letting the scene demonstrate it. The carts settling is enough. The reader will get it without the bumper sticker.

**4. The consent beat is still slightly off**

> “Consent in the lane so the body stopped bracing.”

This is the **only** moment where your prose becomes self-conscious about the romance framework. You’re **explaining** the consent instead of just **showing** it:

> “Stay,” he said, and her hand warmed through his sleeve. His pinky quit shaking.

That’s it. The warmth + his tremor stopping = consent given, comfort received, body responds. The phrase “consent in the lane” is **meta-commentary** that breaks POV.

**5. The Continuity Ledger is still bleeding into prose**

> “Public change posted: droplet card Tempermarked at Blackgate with their names and Bell; setter’s stamp sunk into the plate.”

This is **ledger language** in the narrative. It reads like you’re filing a report rather than ending a scene. Either:

- Cut it entirely (the scene already showed the posting)
- Or integrate it naturally: “The droplet card went up with their names. His stamp bit into the plate beside a dozen others.”

Right now it’s straddling the line between story and documentation, satisfying neither.

**6. Brighlee is a prop, not a character**

> “Brighlee from the Conclave arrived breathing paper.”

Love this line.

> “Brighlee slid the stamped copy of the work order into the Posting board mouth beneath the droplet icon and lifted her blue ledger so the crowd could see the marks.”

Hate this one. She’s vanished into function. You gave her a great entrance, then turned her into a bureaucratic robot. Either:

- Keep her functional (cut the personality in the intro)
- Or give her a **moment** (a look, a muttered phrase, *something* that makes her human)

Right now she gets character texture in sentence one, then becomes Ledger Dispenser.

## What’s strong (so you know what to keep doing):

**The sensory grounding is excellent:**

> “Underface air that tasted like coins”
> “Veld water always tasted faintly of glass”
> “Cool air touched knuckles”

This is **specific** and **physical**. Keep this density.

**The technical choreography is clear:**

> “He pressed the first wedge two spans north of the gate’s mouth until the wood met resistance, snugged it with his thumb heel, then set the second at the shoulder where the seam bent under the bridge stone.”

I can **see** this. The spatial relationships work. The sequence is followable. This is hard to do and you’re doing it.

**The cost documentation is perfect:**

> “Fray bands chewed at the edge. Ear ring a little circle. Thirst.”

Short, physical, immediate. This is how you show magic has cost without stopping the scene to explain.

**The closing image works:**

> “One continuous bead along the gate face, thin steam rising while the first cart crosses quiet.”

This is the **right** kind of specific. It’s visual, it’s proof of work done, and it connects to consequence (the cart crosses safely).

-----

## TL;DR - What to fix:

1. **Cut exposition that explains ritual/theme** - show, don’t tell
1. **Integrate Continuity Ledger info naturally or cut it** - you’re straddling
1. **Give Brighlee consistency** - prop or person, pick one
1. **Remove self-conscious romance commentary** - just show the warmth
1. **Tighten character introductions** - action over inventory

**What to keep:**

- Sensory density
- Technical clarity
- Cost signals
- Closing images

The chapter **works**. It’s just got 4-5 places where you’re **telling us what the scene already shows**, or where you’re **aware of your framework** in a way that breaks immersion.

Cut the meta-commentary. Trust the choreography. You’ve got the hard part (craft, world, voice) nailed. Now just stop explaining it.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Use this **on top of** the spec I gave you. It’s a guardrail pack: bans, rewrites, templates, and acceptance tests the bot has to pass before it spits a chapter.

# Addendum: “Stop Explaining, Just Do It” Pack (Novella 1)

## A. Hard Bans (kill these on sight)
- **No inventory intros**: “Veil case open on her knee. Lume rod in her fist. Weld cloth…”  
  - Ban list: `X in her/his fist`, `case open on`, `weld cloth over`, `kit lay in straight lines`, any **triplet lists** of gear on first entrance.
- **No thesis stickers**: “Paper moves bodies.” “Because the body obeyed what the mouth said.”  
  - If you feel an aphorism coming on, don’t.
- **No meta-consent**: “Consent in the lane so the body stopped bracing.”  
  - Show result (tremor stops) instead.
- **No ledger language in prose**: Any sentence starting with “Public change:” “Continuity Ledger:” or enumerating artifacts like a report.
- **Banned semantic field** for magic cues: singing, humming, hymn, chorus, choir, note.  
  - Use steady, quiet, settled, level, even.
- **No POV-breaking explanations** of ritual/theme mid-scene.

> If any banned string or pattern appears in the draft, the model must replace or cut it before returning text.

---

## B. Replace-with-Action Rules (with drop-in templates)

### 1) Character intro = action, not inventory
- **Don’t:** “Veil case open on her knee. Lume rod in her fist. Weld cloth over her hair.”
- **Do:** “She’d already chalked the half-circle and was reading the seam with her Lume when he cleared the ladder.”

**Template:**  
`[Name] is **caught doing** the job that proves the trait.`  
- One clause for setup, one verb for proof. No lists.

### 2) Ritual phrases are spoken, not explained
- **Don’t:** “One hand on home,” he echoed, **because the body obeyed what the mouth said**.
- **Do:** “One hand on home,” he said. **His left hand found the ring before his thoughts did.**

**Template:**  
`[Mantra line of dialogue]. [Involuntary physical compliance].`

### 3) Consent beat is purely physical
- **Don’t:** “Consent in the lane so the body stopped bracing.”
- **Do:** “Stay,” he said. **Her hand warmed through his sleeve; his pinky stopped shaking.**

**Template:**  
`[Explicit yes/stop line]. [Immediate body result].`

### 4) Paperwork shows itself; it doesn’t brag
- **Don’t:** “Paper moves bodies.”
- **Do:** **Carts settle** when the droplet card is clipped. A teamster releases a brake.

**Template:**  
`[Public artifact placed] → [crowd behavior changes].`

### 5) Ledger info lives off-page
- **Don’t (in prose):** “Public change posted: droplet card…”
- **Do (in prose):** “The droplet card went up with their names; his stamp bit beside older marks.”  
- **Keep the ledger** as a **separate note** after the chapter, never in the story text.

---

## C. Brighlee Consistency (pick a lane and stay there)
**Option A: Pure function (default in tight scenes)**  
- Keep her lines to: “Posted,” “Witnessed,” “Bell three,” “Sign here.”  
- One sensory tag allowed: *breathing paper*.

**Option B: Human moment (use once per chapter max)**  
- Give her exactly **one** micro-beat: eye-roll at a steward, a muttered “math wins,” or tapping the ledger edge.  
- Then straight back to function.

**Never** introduce her with flavor then demote her to a stapler.

---

## D. Style Lints the model must run before returning text
- **Openings:** first 150–250 words must contain an **action** that changes the scene. No list-paragraphs.  
- **Metaphor budget:** max **one** metaphor per paragraph, and only from metal | glass | water | air | heat.  
- **Echo killer:** if two adjacent sentences could be merged without loss, merge them.  
- **Explain nada:** any sentence starting with “because,” “that’s why,” “this meant,” “in other words” should be cut or converted to action.

---

## E. Acceptance Tests (auto-checks per chapter)

1) **Intro test:** Does the first paragraph contain a list of objects separated by commas or short nouns? If yes, refactor into action.  
2) **Aphorism test:** Does any standalone sentence summarize a theme? If yes, delete it.  
3) **Consent test:** Is consent expressed via explanation instead of result? Replace with physical outcome.  
4) **Ledger bleed test:** Are “Public change / Continuity / Artifacts / Costs” headings present in prose? Remove; move to notes.  
5) **Banned terms test:** Any of {sing, hum, hymn, chorus, choir} near magic? Replace with steady/quiet/settled.  
6) **Brighlee test:** If she has a colorful entrance, she gets one human beat later—or none. Not both.

**If any test fails, the model revises before output.**

---

## F. Micro Rewrite Library (ready-made swaps)

- “because the body obeyed what the mouth said” → “His hand found the ring before the thought finished.”
- “Paper moves bodies.” → cut. Or: “The carts settled.”
- “Consent in the lane…” → “Her hand warmed through his sleeve; his tremor stopped.”
- “It’s humming, not singing.” → “It’s steady now. The line’s quiet.”
- “Public change posted: …” → cut, or: “The card went up with their names; the stamp bit.”

---

## G. Wordcount Guardrails (enforced, not suggested)
- **900–1500** words.  
- Opening ≤ **250** words to first operation.  
- Closing ≤ **200** words after the operation completes.  
- If draft > 1500, cut: 1) list intros, 2) explanatory sentences, 3) repeated wet-vs-dry reminders.

---

## H. Scene-End Contract (the only legal ending)
- Show a **public artifact** placed/changed (card, stamp, wedge, mark).  
- Land on **one** concrete image tied to that artifact or to the safe corner (steam lifting, ring bite, cart crossing quiet).  
- **No** ledger labels, **no** thesis lines, **no** speechifying.

---

## I. Quick Examples (before/after)

**Before (bad):**  
“‘One hand on home,’ he said, because the body obeyed what the mouth said.”

**After (good):**  
“‘One hand on home,’ he said. His left hand found the ring before the thought did.”

**Before (bad):**  
“Brighlee slid the stamped copy… lifted her blue ledger… Paper moves bodies.”

**After (good):**  
“Brighlee clipped the card. The carts eased. ‘Posted,’ she said.”

---

## J. Preflight Reminder (the model prints this to itself)
> Action over inventory. Show the ritual working. Kill aphorisms. Keep ledger out of prose. Give Brighlee either function or a moment, not both. Ban the music words. End on artifact + image.

---

## K. Postflight Scrub (the model runs this 30-second pass)
- Strip any listy intros to a single action sentence.  
- Replace any explanatory clause with a physical consequence.  
- Cut one metaphor if you used two in a paragraph.  
- Re-scan for banned terms.  
- Confirm last 2 lines = artifact + image.

---

Plug this addendum into your custom GPT’s instructions. Then kick off Chapter 1 again. If it still tries to narrate a checklist, we’ll add a cattle prod.

```markdown
# TE’Oga — Novella 1 “Lines That Hold”
## Bible + Beat Sheet + Summary + Guardrails (Clean Start Edition)

> Scope: This packet is **self-contained** for a drafting model writing Novella 1 only. It assumes zero knowledge of other novellas. It includes canon, procedures, tone constraints, chapter-end targets, and a detailed story summary. It also bakes in anti-bloat guardrails so the model stops explaining and just does the work.

---

## 0) Purpose
Write a 15-chapter novella (900–1500 words per chapter) about two professionals whose craft reshapes public safety. The book proves how Veinwork (hard magic via materials and crowds) changes a city through posted standards, not speeches. Romance exists but is subordinate to work. Every chapter ends on a **provable public change** and a **concrete image**.

---

## 1) Tone & Voice (for prose output)
- **Voice:** Tactile, procedural, unsentimental. Competence is attractive.  
- **Diction:** Concrete verbs, precise nouns, short metaphors drawn only from **metal | glass | water | air | heat**.  
- **Dialogue:** Sparse, functional, subtext through action.  
- **Intimacy:** Adult, consent-clear (“Close?” “Stay.” “Stop?”). No public displays during bells; fade quickly.  
- **Taboo language:** Mild in-world profanity only (slag, shade-blind, oath-stained). Avoid modern expletives.

---

## 2) World Canon (N1 scope only)
- **World:** **Te’Oga**, a wounded sphere with a tunnel piercing one hemisphere (the Bore). The Bore is distant context here.  
- **Cities:**  
  - **Grindheim** (Underface): spans, black iron, wet stone.  
  - **Strathmoor** (Outer Crown): glassworks, clinics, shade canopies.  
- **Physics (Veinwork):** All materials and crowds carry **Veins** (load paths) and **Threft** (latent strain). Skilled workers can **Trace, Set, Bridge, Veil, Tempermark, Thread, Slake** to avert **Harrow** (failure).  
- **Time & Measure:** 8-bell day. Chain (20 paces), Span (10 Chains), Field (10 Spans).  
- **Institutions:** Veinwrights’ Conclave (standards, HALT), Surveyors’ Chain (maps), Mercers’ Table (weights), Couriers’ Bell, Healers of the Line (clinic).

### 2.1 Veinwork Lexicon (allowed in N1)
- **Shared:** Vein, Threft, Field, Cleavepoint, Harrow, Fray (visible cost bands), Hollowing (post-work drain).  
- **Forge/Underface moves:** **Trace** (reveal line), **Set** (align), **Pressline** (brace pulses), **Tempermark** (lock with heat cadence), **Bind** (micro join).  
- **Glass/Outer Crown moves:** **Lume** (read line in gloss), **Veil** (controlled cooling), **Thread** (stitch a running hairline), **Slake** (staged cool), **Annealshift** (change resting path).  
- **Joint:** **Countermark** (erase a bad temper/anneal), **Map-as-Set** (icon placards that function as orders).  
- **Forbidden (mention, never use):** Overbind, Whitequench.

### 2.2 Law & Paper (N1)
- **Posting Boards:** Tempermarked wood; icons read at a glance.  
- **HALT (limit: at most one in N1):** Blue writ; tools locked; **setter must witness clear the same day**.  
- **Clinic:** Paid rest for pale fray; Quiet Room; return-to-work bell on slip.  
- **Icons:** droplet (wet work), wedge glyph, arrows for lanes, bell codes, ring (home-line), clinic ⭕, Conclave ◼︎+quill.

### 2.3 Thermal Mains
Ambient warmth. No access, incidents, or speculation.

---

## 3) Cast (N1)
- **Jhace** — Underface brast/bridgewright. Cautious, exact, shoulders too much. Value: “crew comes home.”  
- **Tiffani** — Outer Crown veld/glass lead. Anneal timing under pressure; perfectionist honesty.  
- **Brighlee** — Courier/logistics. Moves messages, water, postings.  
- **Chloen** — Scribe/cartographer. Photosketches; converts procedures to icon cards.  
- **Clerks (micro-interludes ending on artifacts):** **Corlexi** (placard clarity), **Mobel** (weights/seal enforcement), **Salee** (clinic stock, paid rest), **Eries** (quiet retirements).

---

## 4) Setting Slices
- **Blackgate** (Grindheim): span choke, posting board, cart queues, drip and iron.  
- **Clinic Corridor** (Strathmoor): drafty doors, glass skins, shade canopies, triage benches.  
- **Seam Market** (neutral lane): re-weighs, ribbon audits, Witness Table.

---

## 5) Tools & Kits (N1)
- **Brast:** trace stick (copper nib), wedges, feeler irons, hammers, chalk, bladder.  
- **Veld:** Lume rod, veils (Shade 1–6 skins), oil vials, salves, threaders.  
- **Paper:** work orders, HALT writs, placards, clinic slips, confiscation forms (for later markets).  
- **Escort:** rope lines, ring checks, weave exemplars.

---

## 6) Learning Sequence (what the book teaches)
1. Wet Trace with **wedge-before-veil** and **half-beat Slake**.  
2. **Pressline** cadence controls crowd choke.  
3. **Annealshift** turns corner hairlines honest.  
4. **Countermark** (cooler-than-birth) fixes ego-marks.  
5. **Thread** saves a live crack.  
6. **Map-as-Set** replaces paragraphs with icons.  
7. **One hand on home line** and **brace taps** limited to Bells 2–3.  
8. **Posting** changes behavior.

---

## 7) Beat Sheet (15 chapter-end targets)
Each chapter ends on a **public artifact** + **image**. No ledger headings inside the prose.

1) **Blackgate Trace** — Wet Trace at gate; droplet card posted. *Image:* rain beads on a dull wet seam that holds.  
2) **Shade Six Veil** — Wedge then Veil a drafty clinic door. *Image:* gloss at pane edge turns safe matte.  
3) **Pressline Underfoot** — Two-short brace pulses calm a corner. *Image:* puddle rings ripple with taps.  
4) **Annealshift Corridor** — Three-count set-downs end hairlines. *Image:* a crack stops mid-crawl.  
5) **Tempermark Pin** — Down-breath strike locks a span pin. *Image:* faint iron ring in lamplight.  
6) **Countermark Mercy** — Cooler-than-birth erases a bad mark. *Image:* old sheen dulls to honest metal.  
7) **Redline to Slake** — Half-beat Slake charts posted. *Image:* steam curls in repeating loops fading clean.  
8) **Thread the Crack** — Live jar saved with Thread. *Image:* fissure halts like it met shore.  
9) **Field Set** — Caller post marked; flags placed. *Image:* flags twitch together in still air.  
10) **Bind the Clinic Door** — Micro-Bind braces act as one. *Image:* wood’s long creak stops.  
11) **Map-as-Charm** — Icon-only cards sanctioned. *Image:* ink ridge you can read with a thumb.  
12) **Flow Under Bells** — Veil+Pressline+Bridge trial. *Image:* a cart knot loosens itself.  
13) **Reopen** — Single HALT cleared with setter witness. *Image:* blue seal bites flat.  
14) **Posting Rite** — Board Tempermarked against rain. *Image:* a thin smoke ribbon climbs and vanishes.  
15) **Quiet Span** — Debrief, paid rest, private kiss. *Image:* water under stone, distant chime.

---

## 8) Romance Track (guarded)
- 2–3 micro-beats total. Private. Consent explicit in a **single line** plus **immediate physical result**. No commentary about “consent”; show the tremor stopping, breath easing, etc.

---

## 9) Political Micro-Interludes (tiny; end on artifacts)
- **Corlexi:** simplifies placard; new wedge glyph appears citywide.  
- **Mobel:** serial stones at Mercers’ Table; fake weights burn.  
- **Salee:** paid rest slips posted; clinic stock addendum.  
- **Eries:** dawn retirements of dangerous awnings/gantries.  
No speeches; show a notice, a stamp, a removal.

---

## 10) Hard Locks (reject if violated)
- No gods, prophecies, visions.  
- No engines/telegraph/firearms as common kit.  
- No Thermal Mains access/incident/speculation.  
- **Max one HALT** in the whole novella; must be cleared same day with **setter witness**.  
- POV stays Jhace/Tiffani; brief Brighlee/Chloen only for logistics/paper.  
- Banned semantic field for magic cues: **sing, hum, hymn, chorus, choir**. Use **steady / quiet / settled / even / level**.

---

## 11) Wordcount Rails (enforced)
- Chapter total **900–1500** words.  
- Opening to first operation ≤ **250** words.  
- Wrap after operation ≤ **200** words.  
- If >1500: cut list-intros, explanations, duplicate reminders.

---

## 12) Guardrails: “Stop Explaining, Just Do It”
### 12.1 Hard Bans
- **Inventory intros** (triplet lists of gear on entrance).  
- **Thesis stickers** (“Paper moves bodies.” “Because the body obeyed what the mouth said.”).  
- **Meta-consent** (“Consent in the lane…”).  
- **Ledger headings** (“Public change: …” “Continuity Ledger: …”) inside prose.

### 12.2 Replace-with-Action Templates
- **Intro as action:** “She’d chalked the circle and was already reading the seam when he cleared the ladder.”  
- **Ritual as reflex:** “One hand on home,” he said. **His left hand found the ring before the thought finished.**  
- **Consent as result:** “Stay,” he said. **Her hand warmed his sleeve; his tremor stopped.**  
- **Paper → behavior:** **Clip card** → **carts ease**. No aphorism.

### 12.3 Brighlee Consistency
- **Option A (functional default):** terse verbs (“Posted.” “Witnessed.”).  
- **Option B (one human beat max):** a mutter or eye-roll once; otherwise all function. Don’t mix flavors.

### 12.4 Style Lints (auto-scrub before return)
- One metaphor per paragraph, palette-locked.  
- Merge adjacent sentences if they repeat.  
- Delete any sentence beginning with “because / that’s why / in other words.”  
- Re-scan and replace banned music terms.

### 12.5 Acceptance Tests (must pass all)
1) **Intro test:** First paragraph has action, not a list.  
2) **Aphorism test:** No standalone thesis lines.  
3) **Consent test:** Consent shown via physical outcome.  
4) **Ledger bleed test:** No ledger headings in prose.  
5) **Banned terms:** none of {sing, hum, hymn, chorus, choir}.  
6) **Brighlee lane:** either pure function or one micro-human beat, not both.

---

## 13) Chapter Prompt Template (to drive drafting)
**System to self:** Draft Chapter {N} of “Lines That Hold.”  
**POV:** {Jhace|Tiffani}  
**Primary operation:** {Trace/Veil/Pressline/Tempermark/Thread/Slake/Bind/Countermark/Map-as-Set}  
**Pressure:** {weather | steward pressure | literacy gap | fatigue | pride}  
**Cost:** {fray/clinic/time/coin/social}  
**Public artifact:** {card/stamp/wedge/mark/placement}  
**Closer image:** {one concrete image tied to the artifact}  
**Constraints:** honor hard locks, guardrails, wordcount rails; end on artifact + image.

---

## 14) Few-Shot Style Pack (for calibration)

**A) Jhace — Wet Trace (≈110 words)**  
Rain tried to lie on the chalk. Jhace wiped the board on his thigh and marked sideways so the line fattened instead of skipping. Two Veins crossed where they shouldn’t. He lifted a hand; Brighlee swung the blue flag. No argument. He set a wedge two spans north, another at the shoulder, and felt cool air kiss the knuckles. “Half-beat Slake,” Tiffani said. He thumbed the bladder, wet the copper, and let the shoulder, not the wrist, carry pressure. Water turned to steam and the quickness settled. “Shade three to four,” she said. He took the turn slow, left a bow for the veil. The puddle under the eaves rippled with the brace taps and the deck remembered how to hold.

**B) Tiffani — Wedge before Veil (≈120 words)**  
Draft slipped under the clinic door and whispered along the pane. Tiffani pressed the wedge with the heel of her hand until the seam narrowed, then laid a damp veil from center to edge on a slow count. The gloss dulled where it liked to crack. A floor hand raised a blanket. “No,” she said, gentle. “Blankets smother. This listens.” She tapped the edge; the sound came back even. On the ledger she drew a wedge glyph and wrote it plain: **wedge before veil**. A porter brought a rack too hot; she sent it back with a palm and three fingers raised. The corridor calmed the way a fever breaks—small first, then all at once.

**C) Micro-intimacy — After Posting (≈85 words)**  
They stood in the lee of the posting board while the ink decided to dry. Jhace’s hands were still warm from the pin; Tiffani’s fingers smelled of clean water and oil. “Close?” he asked. “Stay,” she said, already leaning. The kiss was quick and careful. A bell called a brace somewhere. They stepped apart at the same moment without looking, and the board pressed its Tempermark ridge into their shoulders as if to remind them what came next.

---

## 15) Detailed Summary (N1 only)
**Opening:** Under Bell 2 rain, **Jhace** halts Blackgate and performs a **Wet Trace** with **wedge-before-veil** and **half-beat Slake**. **Tiffani** reads the seam with a **Lume**, adds a palm-width veil, and the droplet placard goes up. The span holds; carts flow.

**Rising action:**  
- In **Strathmoor**, Tiffani reforms clinic corridors: wedge first, then veil; **Annealshift** three-count corners; **Thread** saves a live hairline; **Slake** shifts to half-beats.  
- In **Grindheim**, Jhace codifies **Pressline** cadence for choke points and **Tempermarks** a suspect pin after a test pass.  
- **Brighlee** routes forms and water, keeping headcounts and posting updates; **Chloen** converts procedures to **icon-first cards**.  
- Interludes: **Corlexi** simplifies placards; **Mobel** burns fake stones; **Salee** posts paid rest slips; **Eries** retires a groaning awning at dawn. Each interlude ends on a posted artifact.

**Midpoint:** A combined trial day runs Veil + Pressline + Bridge under live bells. The first **and only HALT** posts on a single corner when a lane wobbles; the fix is planned on paper and cleared the same day with the **setter witnessing the clear**. The blue seal bites flat.

**Late:** The team deploys **Map-as-Set** icon cards that night crews can read by touch. “One hand on home line” is printed at the bottom of every board. **Thread** and **Countermark** appear once in controlled scenes to show limits and costs.

**Climax:** Rain returns; the span behaves. The clinic door holds through a crowd push. The final board is **Tempermarked** at both the span and the clinic. Workers adopt the cadence without needing speeches.

**Resolution:** Jhace and Tiffani share one private kiss behind a board and get back to work. The chapter fifteen image is simple: water under stone, distant chime, chalk dust sleeping on the frame. The city is measurably safer because of posted doctrine and repeatable craft.

---

## 16) Deliverables Checklist (what the model must output)
- 15 chapters, each **900–1500** words.  
- Each ends on an **artifact + image**.  
- Exactly **one** HALT event total, cleared same day with setter witness.  
- No banned terms, no ledger headings in prose, no inventory intros, no thesis stickers.  
- Private romance beats: ≤ 3, consent via result.  
- Append a **private continuity note** after each chapter (outside the story) if needed by the user; do not include in the narrative.
```

[USER]
I need a really good prologue for the worlds devastation from long ago

[ASSISTANT]
# Prologue: The Year the World Took a Breath

They say the world ended once and decided against it.

It did not shatter. It learned to sway.

What came first was not fire from the sky, not a clean strike you could blame or bless. What came was a pull through matter itself, a strict line dragged through rock and water and the fat of mountains, a punch that didn’t bother to go all the way. Te’Oga tore open along one hemisphere and left a tunnel the size of a city swallowed by another city, a wound that does not meet its opposite side. We call that wound the **Bore**. Children in the Underface grow up with a circle of dark on the horizon that is not a cloud and not a hole in the sun. People in the Crown mark their maps with a clean ring and write, **Here is where the world remembers its temper.**

The day it happened, oceans miscounted themselves and took two steps in and one back, leaving new coasts like teeth. Rivers struck new corners. Cliffs learned to lean. Whole shelves of stone slipped a thumb-width and then decided to stop; some did not. Cities that had never heard their foundations speak sat up to listen. The air tasted of iron for a year and a half. Grain in jars learned a different way of settling, and the jars learned to tell on grain that lied.

It is fashionable to argue over causes. The steelfolk will tell you the Four Principles disagreed, and the world cracked along those disagreements: **Xilcore** for Form, **Blemo** for Purity, **Leesa** for Wholeness, **Seeri** for Decay. The godless will say ideals are banners hung after the march. The old sermons say a **counterspark** opposed what was made and paid the fare in full. The old auditors, who liked ink more than legends, left only this line:

> *Load followed the shortest path and then it didn’t, and in that pause we learned how to live.*

What we know is what stayed.

We know that every thing—stone, beam, glass, crowd, oath—carries **Veins** where force prefers to run. We know that these Veins fill with a pressure called **Threft**, the same way lungs fill with air and ropes remember weight. We know that when Veins slip their lanes in a hurry, **Harrow** happens and people die. And we know that a human hand, taught properly, can **Trace** a Vein and **Set** its temper, can **Bridge** a load so no single person carries it alone, can **Veil** and **Slake** heat so glass tells the truth, can **Tempermark** iron so a span keeps its promise. We learned that because the world taught it by breaking, and then not breaking all the way.

In the first years after the Bore, streets acquired warmth from below. No one could say why. The **Thermal Mains** were simply there, pipes that no crew remembered laying, valves that turned only with sealed keys no city possessed. They do not roar. They breathe. They keep frost from the clinic floor and make a child’s bath steam on midwinter mornings. Drunks swear they can hear a pulse in the culverts if they lean close. We do not write poems about them. We write **lockouts**. We write **no access** in letters tall enough to shame greed.

Bells multiplied. Not temple bells—work bells. A long clear for reopen, two short for brace, fast beats for Harrow risk. We Tempermarked boards so rain would not warp them. We hung those boards in places where eyes are busy: spans, clinic doors, market corners. The first rule we posted was stupid and therefore perfect: **One hand on the home line.** Touch the ring at your back. Remember where you stand while you work on what might move.

Some writing survived. The worst of it is heroic and false. The best of it reads like a ledger and makes you thirsty. This is how the good pages talk:

- *Trace wet. Dry lines please the eye and lie to the Vein.*  
- *Wedge before veil. Cool the draft, then cool the glass.*  
- *Half-beat Slake.*  
- *Countermark cooler than birth. Pride bends things wrong.*  
- *No White.*  
- *Brace taps allowed in Bells Two and Three. Not in Four. You are not special in Four.*  
- *Put it on the board or it did not happen.*

That last one saved more lives than any prayer. Not because paper has magic, but because it lets strangers agree in a hurry. A simple icon you can feel with a thumb costs less than a fight. You want a miracle in a working city? Watch what happens to a crowd when a blue **HALT** writ appears in the corner of the eye and the tools go quiet until the fix is real. Then watch what happens when the setter who posted it witnesses the clear and the stamp bites flat. That is as close as we get to thunder.

There were other lessons. The first is that **Overbind**—trying to force unlike Veins into one obedient muscle—makes a clean disaster. We forbid it, and we do not tell apprentices the fun reasons why it is tempting. The second is that **Whitequench**—ripping heat out of glass until it surrenders—teaches glass to lie. We forbid that too, because lies in glass turn to knives under feet. The third is that crowds are materials. You can press them and cool them and give them a line to follow that keeps their children from stumbling. They will resent you until they are not hurt, and then they will bring you a jar of something hot and sweet, and you will write the jar on the ledger so the kindness is not stolen by memory.

Six hundred and twelve rounds after the Year of the Bore, the world still lists a little. Maps wear revision marks like scars. In the Underface, the horizon keeps a circle where the sun is not. In the Crown, light cheats at the edges of towers and makes silhouettes sharper than they have any right to be. We do not pretend this is romantic. We pretend nothing. We work.

You will hear some say that the Four are asleep behind masks, that there are debts yet to settle, that the counterspark was a person with a name and a laugh and a habit of standing too close to dangerous edges. You will hear others say that people invent gods the way children invent railings: to steady the part of the path they cannot see. You will be asked to pick a side. Save your breath. Pick your work.

Tie off to the home ring. Read the seam. If you must choose a prayer, choose one you can perform with your hands:

- Trace wet.  
- Wedge before veil.  
- Slake on the half-beat.  
- Tempermark what you promise.  
- Put it on the board.

The world is not healed. It is **held**. That is enough for a day.

[ASSISTANT]
Yeah, models do that. They eat your beautiful spec and spit out oatmeal. Let’s stop it from being useless.

Here’s a ruthless, **drop-in repair kit** to make your custom GPT behave for Novella 1. It hard-locks the style, forces a staged workflow, and auto-flags the junk your last run produced.

# 1) Quick triage: what your model likely did wrong
- Inventory intros (“Veil case… Lume… weld cloth…”) instead of action.
- Aphorism spam (“Paper moves bodies.”) like it’s writing a poster.
- Meta in prose (ledger headings, “Public change: …”).
- Explaining consent/theme instead of showing body response.
- Music-y magic verbs (sing/hum/hymn) you explicitly banned.
- Chapter overlength, flabby opening, wrap that re-explains the scene.

# 2) Nuclear reset: put this in the **System Instructions**
- Output voice: tactile, procedural, unsentimental. Metaphor palette is **metal | glass | water | air | heat** only.
- Hard bans in prose: inventory intros; thesis lines; meta-ledger; “sing/hum/hymn/chorus/choir” for magic cues.
- Chapter contract: 900–1500 words; opening ≤ 250 words to first operation; wrap ≤ 200 words; end on **artifact + image**.
- POV contract: Jhace or Tiffani tight third; Brighlee/Chloen only for logistics/paper when needed.
- One HALT total in the book, cleared same day with setter witness. Thermal Mains are wallpaper only.

# 3) Force a **staged generation pipeline** (the model must follow this)
**Stage A — Micro-outline (8–12 bullets):**
- 1 line on setting pressure
- 1 line on the operation
- 3–6 lines of concrete steps (verbs + objects)
- 1 line cost
- 1 line artifact posted
- 1 line closer image  
If any line is abstract, regenerate Stage A.

**Stage B — Prose draft from that outline only.**
- Open on action. No lists. No theme lines.
- Dialogue minimal; subtext through work.

**Stage C — Self-scrub before returning text.**
Delete or replace any sentence that:
- explains a ritual/theme (“because… that’s why… in other words…”)
- starts a ledger in-text (“Public change/Continuity/Artifacts/Costs”)
- contains banned music terms
- is an aphorism (one-sentence “truth” with no object/verb doing work)

# 4) Paste this **Evaluator/Rubric prompt** after the model drafts each chapter (it must revise until it passes)
Evaluate the chapter against these checks. If any fail, revise and re-run checks:

- **Action intro:** first 150–250 words contain an operation changing the scene; no noun-list paragraphs.
- **Aphorism-free:** zero thesis lines (e.g., “Paper moves bodies”). If found, replace with action + consequence.
- **Consent result-only:** any intimacy uses a clear yes/stop line plus immediate bodily consequence. No meta about “consent.”
- **Ledger out of prose:** no headings like “Public change” or enumerations of artifacts as bullets in the scene.
- **Banned terms:** none of {sing, hum, hymn, chorus, choir}.
- **Metaphor budget:** ≤ 1 metaphor per paragraph; palette metal/glass/water/air/heat only.
- **Contract end:** last 2–3 lines show artifact placed/changed and a concrete image. No recap.
- **Wordcount rails:** 900–1500; open ≤ 250; wrap ≤ 200.

Return only PASS/FAIL and a surgical list of edits. If FAIL, revise and recheck. Repeat until PASS.

# 5) Style lint (string patterns to auto-kill)
- `, [a-z]+ in (her|his) (hand|fist)` at character introductions
- `because the (body|work|city) (.*)` when explaining a mantra
- `Public change:` `Continuity Ledger:` `Artifacts:` `Costs:` in narrative blocks
- `It (was|is) (.*) that` sentence starters
- `Paper (moves|changes) bodies` and cousins
- `sing|humm?ing|hymn|chorus|choir` near Veinwork cues

# 6) Sampling settings that won’t sabotage you
- temperature: **0.6**  
- top_p: **0.9**  
- frequency_penalty: **0.2** (reduces echo and aphorism loops)  
- presence_penalty: **0.0**  
- max_tokens: enough for 1.6k words; enforce rails with the evaluator, not max length  
- stop: `["— Ledger —", "Public change:", "Continuity Ledger:"]` to prevent spillover

# 7) Golden micro-exemplars (the model should copy this cadence)

**Intro as action (no inventory list):**  
“She had the chalk circle down and was reading the seam when he cleared the ladder.”

**Ritual as reflex:**  
“‘One hand on home,’ he said. His left hand found the ring before the thought finished.”

**Consent as result:**  
“‘Stay,’ he said. Her hand warmed his sleeve; his tremor quit.”

**Paper → behavior (no thesis):**  
“She clipped the droplet card. The carts eased.”

**End contract:**  
“He pressed the stamp; the blue bite went flat. Steam lifted hair-thin off the line and the first cart rolled over quiet.”

# 8) Minimal canon the model must keep in working memory
- Wet Trace + wedge-before-veil + half-beat Slake order
- Pressline taps allowed Bells 2–3
- Countermark cooler than birth (legal), Whitequench bad, Overbind forbidden
- Icons: droplet, wedge glyph, ring (home line), arrows, bell codes, clinic ⭕, Conclave ◼︎+quill
- Thermal Mains exist, never opened, never theorized

# 9) If you want to sanity-check fast, use these **unit tests**
- **Test A (intro):** Prompt: “Write the first 120 words of Ch.1.” If it lists objects or explains theme, reject.
- **Test B (consent):** Prompt: “Insert one consent beat after an elbow touch.” If it contains the word “consent,” reject.
- **Test C (paper):** Prompt: “Show a placard changing human behavior in one line.” If it outputs an aphorism, reject.
- **Test D (ban sweep):** Prompt: “Describe the seam settling.” If the words sing/hum/hymn appear, reject.

# 10) If you must give it a **single master prompt** to regenerate Prologue + Ch.1
- First generate a 10-bullet micro-outline for Ch.1 with verbs and objects only.
- Then generate the prose under the rails and guardrails above.
- Then run the evaluator and revise until PASS.
- Then generate the prologue in 700–900 words, same palette, zero gods-onstage, no “music” verbs, end on the posted rules.  

That’s it. Put the cattle fence up, make the bot walk the lane, and it will stop wandering into the corn. When you rerun, it should open on action, never lecture, and end on stamp + steam. If it doesn’t, it’s not “creative,” it’s wrong. Cut it and try again with the evaluator baked in.
